As we wrap-up CS 142, we want to both look back on what we learned this quarter, as well as look forward to where you can go from here, regardless of whether you intend to major/minor in CS or not.

What (we hope) You've Learned¶

Like any university class, we covered a number of topics this quarter: trees, graphs, advanced object-oriented concepts (inheritance, properties, ...), type-checking, functional programming, software design, software testing, etc. However, we hope that what you're taking away from this class is more than just the knowledge of how to implement and use certain data structures, or how to apply certain design principles (although these are certainly very valuable)

Here are a few things we hope you're taking away from this class (and, more broadly, from CS 141-142):

  • Programming requires thinking about problems in a different way. CS 141 introduced you to the notion of computational thinking, which involves thinking about abstraction, modeling, algorithms, and complexity. We hope both classes have reinforced the importance of computational thinking (and how learning to program is more than just "learning a language", and instead requires building a lot of problem-solving skills)

  • Programming is a skill and, like any skill, it requires practice to get better at it. Not just that, it also requires "stepping on the rakes" and learning from your missteps:

    Seasoned programmers are not naturally talented or “born to program”: they’ve just stepped on so many rakes that they’ve gotten really good at avoiding them (but we still step on them now and then... and how!) Or, as a (probably apocryphal) art professor used to tell art school students: “You all have 10,000 bad drawings in you, and our goal is to get through those as quickly as we can”. Similarly, if you’re new to programming, you have a lot of bad lines of code in you, and we've hopefully gotten many of those out of your system.

  • Software development involves a lot more than just programming. In this class, you have gotten your first taste of the software development process, and we hope you are walking away with an appreciation that developing software involves a lot more than just writing code.

  • Software development is a team sport. For most of you, the course project has been your first exposure to working in a software team. We hope this gave you a sense for the rewards and challenges of collaborative software development, which you will encounter in pretty much any real software project (and in many CS classes).

We also encourage you to take a step back and look at the big picture: you are still at the beginning of your journey as programmers, computer scientists, technologists, etc. You are not expected to be a coding virtuoso, the same way that someone who has taken two quarters of piano cannot be expected to book a gig in Carnegie Hall. In case you haven't heard the old joke:

A tourist is visiting New York, and is really excited to visited the renowned Carnegie Hall, where so many distinguished musicians have performed over the years. Alas, they can't find their way around town, so they stop a New Yorker on the street and ask them:

"Excuse me, how do I get to Carnegie Hall?"

The New Yorker replies:

"Practice."

The goal of these introductory classes is to lay a foundation you can build upon. Yes, you can start applying a lot of what you've learned (and we'll provide some suggestions further below), but there is still a lot more you can learn. So, where do we go from here?

What's Next?¶

There are many topics you can learn about after you've built a good foundation in computer science. If you pursue a CS major, you'll sample many different topics; if you're not pursuing a CS major, you may focus on just a few topics.

But what are these topics you could learn about? Let's explore them by thinking about our course project: we've developed a game where the classes are designed to provide a clean separation between the game logic and the user interface, allowing us to use the same game logic to implement a GUI, TUI, and Bot.

But lets say that we wanted to turn this into a real software product with a lot more functionality. Let's say we wanted to take this further and develop an online version of our game that can be played through a web interface, and with the following features:

  • Allows multiple players to play a game through the website (where the players could be connecting from separate locations)
  • Supports multiple games, not just Go.
  • Can be access not just through a website, but also via mobile apps.

Doing so will likely require us to expand our knowledge in various areas of CS. Please note that, while we've included a few representative CS classes offered at UChicago, this is not meant to be a comprehensive listing of the CS curriculum at UChicago. For a more complete picture, take a look at the Computer Science page of the College Catalog.

Software Engineering¶

In this class, we've started to learn about software design and, generally, sensible development practices. To scale this project further, we would need to learn more about the whole software development process, particularly since we would need to meet a much larger set of requirements. Keeping track of all those requirements, designing solutions for them, and managing all the people involved in the project is a non-trivial endeavour.

One aspect of Software Engineering that we've only hinted at is how building complex software systems often involves interacting with many different APIs (Application Programmer Interfaces). For example, when you're using pygame, you're interacting with it through the "pygame API".

Of particular importance in modern systems is the use of online APIs. Let's say we wanted to save the results of each match to a leaderboard. We could roll out our own leaderboard implementation, or we could try to find some sort of "leaderboard library" that we could import into our code. Alternatively, someone might be running an online leaderboard service that we can submit match results to. The way we submit these results is via an API that sends messages to that service over the Internet (this is a further example of a client/server model). Here is one example of a Leaderboard API hosted at Amazon: https://developer.amazon.com/docs/leaderboardsandtournaments/game-api-ref.html (you don't need to understand all the details, but notice how a lot of the operations they offer make sense: Submit Score, Get Leaderboard, etc.)

There are tons of online APIs that you can use to build software. You can find a list of free APIs here: https://github.com/public-apis/public-apis For example, the National Weather Service has a public API that allows you to access weather data in JSON format. Here is the current forecast for Chicago: https://api.weather.gov/gridpoints/LOT/76,68/forecast

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 22000 - Introduction to Software Development
  • CMSC 22001 - Software Construction

Computer Networks¶

Like a lot of modern software, our website would be accessed over the Internet. Computer networks are thus an integral part of modern software, and understanding how the Internet works is very useful to developing that software, and debugging network-related issues.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 23320 - Foundations of Computer Networks
  • CMSC 23360 - Advanced Networks
  • CMSC 23400 - Mobile Computing

Algorithms¶

The algorithms involved in Go (e.g., to compute territories) involve graph algorithms, and other games can involve complex algorithms (e.g., Checkers involves doing recursive tree traversals to figure out all possible moves). We also hinted at a complex algorithm for suggesting moves, the minimax algorithm, that involves recursion and thinking about game trees. So, if we implement additional games, we are likely to deal with a number of algorithmic challenges, and it is important to know how to select the right algorithm for each problem, and to be aware of the complexity of those algorithms (as well as of the code we write)

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 27100 - Discrete Mathematics
  • CMSC 27130 - Honors Discrete Mathematics
  • CMSC 27200 - Theory of Algorithms
  • CMSC 27230 - Honors Theory of Algorithms

Machine Learning/AI¶

What if we wanted to build more elaborate game-playing bots, such as a bot that is able to learn from a user's moves, and adapt to their playing style? Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence can come in handy for this.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 25300 Mathematical Foundations of Machine Learning
  • CMSC 25400 Machine Learning

Programming Languages¶

Writing a complex algorithm for a bot or AI could be implemented more cleanly in a programming language with a more mathematical flavor, and we might learn about languages that have strengths tailored for specific applications.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 22100 - Programming Languages
  • CMSC 22300 - Functional Programming

Databases¶

In the course project, all our data is ephemeral: the moment we stop our GUI, TUI, etc., all the information in that program is lost. If we're building a website that allows users to create accounts, join games, etc., we'll want to store information about players, matches, tournaments, etc. We can do so with databases, some of which are capable of scaling to storing (and efficiently retrieving) huge volumes of data.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 23500 - Introduction to Database Systems
  • CMSC 23530 - Advanced Database Systems

Security¶

The moment we start sending any sort of data (particularly personal data) over the Internet, or store that data somewhere, we'll need to be mindful of keeping that data secure. We don't want anyone to be able to see someone else's account data, nor do we want anyone to be able to snoop in on messages that are being sent to the server.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 23200 - Introduction to Computer Security
  • CMSC 28400 - Introduction to Cryptography

HCI and UI/UX¶

We built some simple user interfaces for our games, but there is an entire field of CS dedicated to the study of human-computer interactions (HCI). A relate field is User Interface / User Experience (UI/UX) design.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 20300 - Introduction to Human-Computer Interaction

Distributed Systems¶

What if we end up having 1,000,000 users? Surely a single server won't be enough. This is when we have to scale up to running our service across multiple servers, which poses a unique set of challenges, like how we can deal with failures in the servers or ensure that data across the servers is consistent. This is the purview of the field of Distributed Systems.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 23310 - Advanced Distributed Systems

Computer Architecture and Operating Systems¶

Our company could grow to the point where we want to use custom hardware and systems instead of off-the-shelf systems. Understanding how computer hardware works internally, and how operating systems allow software to run on top of that hardware, can be very useful when working with computer systems at that level.

Relevant CMSC classes

  • CMSC 22200 - Computer Architecture
  • CMSC 23000 - Operating Systems

The Importance of Breadth¶

We've framed the discussion above on specific ways we could improve our course project and, while you will likely gravitate towards specific areas of CS as you move further in your CS education, you should not neglect the importance of breadth: many of the topics above are cross-cutting and intertwined in many ways, and gaining exposure to as many of these topics can help you become a better developer/researcher/scientist/etc. as you'll be able to more easily see how elements of one field may be helpful when you're solving a problem in another field.

In other words, you should avoid the mindset of not taking a class because you think you'll never use anything from that class in your career. Think of the CS major like a buffet where you make a first pass and sample a bit of everything, and then come back for seconds to focus on the things you really enjoyed.

How To Become a Better Programmer¶

All the above is good and well, but what can you do to become a better programmer in the more immediate future?

As we've emphasized many times, programming is a skill that requires practice. It's also like learning to speak a new language: you can memorize all the syntax and grammar, but you're only going to get good at it if you practice, practice, practice. Not just that, there's only so much you can gain from talking to yourself; you need to "talk" with others (i.e., you have to work with other developers, or write code that is going to be used by others)

There are a few ways you can do this:

  • Take CS 143! This class will continue to provide a structured environment to learn more about programming and, specifically, systems programming in C, a much lower-level language than Python. Even if you have no intention of becoming a CS major, learning about C can be a formative experience, as it will require building a solid understanding of many low-level concepts that Python abstracts away for us (such as memory management)

  • Be curious. As you take more classes, try to look beyond just the content that is being covered in. Your professors and TAs often know a lot more than what they happen to be covering in class, and are more than happy to share that knowledge with you.

    • Related to this point, take every opportunity possible to learn about the sorts of “supporting” tools or technologies that aren't often taught explicitly in courses. CS curricula don't include classes on "the Linux terminal", "using package managers", "using Git", etc. (although we've sneaked in a lot of those skills throughout the quarter). A programmer's toolbox includes more than just knowing how to code, and learning about all of these supporting tools can be incredibly valuable (but it requires spending some time to get more practice in these skills and tools that are often only mentioned briefly in the context of a larger class).

    • In summary: when someone mentions a new or unfamiliar tool, technology, piece of software, etc, take note and explore it later -- it might just help make your work that much easier.

  • Apply to be a TA or grader for a class you've enjoyed. Besides building many useful skills (explaining complex concepts to others, reviewing other people's code, giving feedback, etc.), being a TA/grader in a class can help you solidify concepts you've learned previously. You will also get a lot of practice debugging and solving problems, which will make you a better programmer.

  • Work on a personal project that motivates you. This can be a good way of challenging yourself to learn about new concepts and Python libraries, and to practice your skills with a project you feel invested in (as opposed to homeworks or projects that are assigned to you in a class).

    • Even better, find a couple of friends who want to work on a project with you! Besides building more collaborative skills, it's easier to stay motivated if you're working with others. You can also participate in events like hackathons and game jams, which can provide a great way to work on a fun project with a team in a short time frame.

  • If you don't have a specific coding project in mind, you can also get some practice on a variety of websites:

    • Python Practice Problems: A collection of Python practice problems we prepared for CS 141/142.

    • Advent of Code: A fun competition where, like an advent calendar, you work on a new coding challenge each day. Many of the problems (especially the first ones) are accessible to beginner programmers, and can be solved in any language. Even though the competition itself happens in December, all their past problems are available on their site (and you can work on them at any time)

    • Kattis contains a huge collection of coding problems, and their website allows you to submit solutions and (similar to Gradescope) get an instant score based on a series of automated tests.

    • Another website (more focused on learning and practicing coding skills) you may want to check out is Leetcode.

    • Those of you who are more mathematically-inclined may want to check out Project Euler.

  • Contribute to an open source project. GitHub actually provides a useful guide on How to Contribute to Open Source. You may also want to check out Google Summer of Code (Google pays you over the summer to contribute to an open source project, with the supervision of a mentor from that project).

  • If you are hoping to eventually work in tech, or work in some sort of coding-related position, make your code available on GitHub (not your code from the homeworks or course project, though; please don't post that publicly anywhere!) Having code available for others to freely review is a big selling point when applying to tech jobs. You may also want to check out the GitHub Student Developer Pack.

  • Stay connected with the CS community on campus:

    • Join our Slack Workspace: https://cs-uchicago.slack.com/signup
    • Join our Discord server: https://discord.gg/FYq9kKJ
    • Join the CS undergrads mailing list: https://lists.uchicago.edu/web/info/cs-undergrads
    • Learn about our student organizations (see these slides from an info session on CS/Tech-focused student organizations)
    • The CS department runs an informal weekly event called Hack Night every Friday from 5pm to 8pm (typically in Crerar 390). Hack Night is just three hours where you can hang out with other students interested in technology or computer science or just enjoy some free food while you work on homework or personal projects. It's also a great way to connect with other students interested in CS/tech. You can read more about Hack Night in this flyer.
